questions.scenario:
After a deploy, the log group fills with repeated errors:

```text
09:40:03 INFO release=2026-05-02.4 starting service
09:40:05 ERROR Cannot find module ./dist/server.js
09:40:07 INFO container exiting code=1
09:40:31 ERROR target unhealthy
09:41:02 ERROR service unavailable
09:41:31 ERROR service unavailable
```

Which line is the first useful error?

options.text: `Cannot find module ./dist/server.js`, because it names the startup failure before repeated symptoms.
`service unavailable`, because it appears most often.
`target unhealthy`, because ALB health always causes missing app files.
`starting service`, because info logs are always the root cause.

questions.explanation: The useful clue is the first specific failure that changes the next check. Missing `./dist/server.js` points toward image build, artifact path, or startup command. The later messages are symptoms after the app failed to start.

questions.scenario:
A focused search around one failed request returns:

```text
09:42:16 INFO request_id=req_A order_id=ord_9 checkout.start
09:42:17 WARN request_id=req_A step=dynamodb.put_idempotency_key error_name=ConditionalCheckFailedException
09:42:17 ERROR request_id=req_A step=checkout.abort reason=duplicate_request
09:42:18 ERROR request_id=req_B step=rds.insert_order error_name=ConnectionTimeout
```

For `req_A`, what should the engineer investigate first?

options.text: The idempotency or duplicate-request path, because the first specific failure for `req_A` is DynamoDB conditional check failure.
The RDS timeout from `req_B`, because any later error in the log group belongs to the same request.
S3 lifecycle, because duplicate requests always mean an object expired.
ALB certificate renewal, because checkout aborts are normally TLS problems.

questions.explanation: Request IDs keep the search scoped. For `req_A`, the first useful error is the DynamoDB conditional check, which may be expected duplicate protection or a client retry issue. A different request's RDS timeout should not steer this investigation.

questions.scenario:
A Lambda side job fails after checkout succeeds:

```text
START RequestId: 4f0a
INFO service=devpolaris-receipt-email request_id=req_52 order_id=ord_52 step=email.render
ERROR service=devpolaris-receipt-email request_id=req_52 order_id=ord_52 step=email.render error_name=TemplateNotFound locale=en-GB
REPORT RequestId: 4f0a Duration: 92 ms Max Memory Used: 89 MB
```

What is the first useful diagnosis path?

options.text: Check the receipt template configuration or deployment artifact for locale `en-GB`.
Increase Lambda memory first, because the report line mentions memory.
Resize RDS, because every Lambda error after checkout means the order table is full.
Delete the Lambda log group, because platform lines are too noisy to keep.

questions.explanation: The app log names the failing step and error: missing template for a locale. The platform report is useful context, but the memory value is not the failure here. Follow the first specific app error.

questions.scenario: Which habits help find a first useful error in CloudWatch Logs without getting lost?

options.text: Start with the service log group and the incident time window.
Filter by request ID, order ID, route, or `ERROR` when those fields are available.
Prefer the first specific dependency or assumption failure over later repeated symptoms.
Start by reading every stream from every environment before choosing a request.

questions.explanation: A narrow search keeps the investigation calm. Request IDs, time windows, and structured fields reduce noise. The first specific failure usually teaches more than repeated downstream symptoms.

questions.scenario:
The team reviews a real outage and finds this was the only application error:

```text
09:42:18 ERROR failed
```

The rest of the evidence had to come from incomplete clues, because there was no request ID, route, step, dependency, or error name. What should the follow-up action be?

options.text: Improve application logging with safe structured fields that name request, route, step, dependency, and error category.
Stop sending logs to CloudWatch, because one vague line proves logs are not useful.
Keep the line as-is and add more alarms for the word `failed`.
Move all order data into logs so future incidents have every value in one place.

questions.explanation: A vague log line is an application logging problem, not a reason to abandon logs. The fix is a small set of safe, consistent fields. Future engineers need enough context to find the first clue without exposing sensitive data.
